does: This paper introduces On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT), a method for improving language model safety by training the model to produce consistent outputs across contrastive input pairs. Unlike prior consistency training approaches that generate supervision offline and apply supervised fine-tuning, OPCT computes the consistency objective dynamically over the model's own responses, with the model learning to align its outputs when given semantically equivalent prompts with different safety framings. The method is evaluated across three safety dimensions—sycophancy, jailbreak resistance, and safety awareness—across multiple model families, demonstrating substantial improvements (reducing sycophancy from 8.1% to 1%) while maintaining or even enhancing model capabilities compared to offline approaches.
connects: This work builds on the emerging consistency training paradigm for alignment, which itself extends supervised fine-tuning approaches by introducing contrastive learning signals to enforce behavioral invariants. The paper directly addresses limitations of offline consistency training methods by adopting on-policy learning, drawing inspiration from reinforcement learning traditions where models learn from their own generated samples rather than fixed datasets. It engages with longstanding challenges in LLM alignment including sycophancy and jailbreak susceptibility, problems previously tackled through reward modeling and constitutional AI approaches. The emphasis on maintaining capabilities while improving safety responds to observed overfitting and capability regression in standard alignment methods.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more sample-efficient and adaptive alignment methods that leverage the model's own generative capabilities during training. OPCT's success with on-policy objectives suggests future work combining consistency training with broader reinforcement learning frameworks for alignment. The framework could extend to additional safety axes and failure modes beyond those studied, and the technique may generalize to other domains requiring behavioral consistency across input variations. The capability-preserving nature of the approach could enable scaling alignment methods to larger models where capability degradation carries higher costs, and could facilitate research into understanding what invariants models learn when trained this way.
